themes.title: Curious Scientists
themes.description: Words for observing, testing, and explaining

words.example: We observe how the seedlings change.
The footprints were evidence of an animal.

words.definition: to say what may happen next
words.example: I predict the ice will melt.

words.definition: an organized way of doing something
words.example: Their method kept the test fair.
We conclude that light helps plants grow.

themes.title: Story Builders
themes.description: Words for vivid characters and events

words.example: Mia was reluctant to enter the cave.
We were fortunate to find shelter.
The lost map creates the story's conflict.
The fog seemed to vanish.
The friends resolve their argument.

themes.title: Roots Reveal Meaning
themes.description: Greek and Latin roots that unlock academic words

words.example: Her answer was barely audible from the back row.

words.definition: to carry from one place to another
words.example: Trucks transport food to the market.
Inspect the model before turning it in.

words.definition: to state what is likely to happen
words.example: Use the pattern to predict the next term.

words.definition: the written story of a person's life
words.example: We read a biography of the inventor.

themes.title: Poetry & Figurative Language
themes.description: Words for discussing images, sounds, and comparisons

words.definition: a direct comparison between unlike things
words.example: The phrase time is a thief is a metaphor.

words.definition: a pattern of beats or sounds
words.example: The poem has a steady rhythm.

words.definition: language that appeals to the senses
words.example: The author uses imagery of cold silver rain.

words.definition: a grouped set of lines in a poem
words.example: The final stanza changes the mood.

words.definition: to explain the meaning of something
words.example: Readers may interpret the symbol differently.

themes.title: Changing Earth
themes.description: Words for systems and natural change

words.definition: the wearing away of rock or soil
words.example: Rain caused erosion along the trail.
Sediment can accumulate near a river bend.
Heat can transform the material.
Water moves through a continuous cycle.
The storm had a major impact.

themes.title: Community & Change
themes.description: Words for people, choices, and society

words.example: Students advocate for a safer crossing.
The school adopted a recycling policy.
Neighbors cooperate on the garden.
Voting is a civic responsibility.

words.definition: a particular way of viewing something
words.example: Listen to each person's perspective.

themes.title: Word Parts at Work
themes.description: Affixes and roots for reading unfamiliar words

words.definition: to state the opposite of another claim
words.example: The new evidence may contradict the first report.
We used a portable microscope outdoors.

words.definition: the people who watch or listen
words.example: Adjust your explanation for the audience.

words.definition: to put completely under a liquid
words.example: Submerge the sample in water.
List the events in chronological order.

themes.title: Literature & Tone
themes.description: Words for analyzing narrators, mood, and craft

words.definition: the voice that tells a story
words.example: The narrator knows only one character's thoughts.

words.definition: uncertainty or excitement about what may happen
words.example: Short sentences build suspense.
The bridge may symbolize change.
Contrast the two characters' responses.

words.definition: unexpected in a way that highlights a difference
words.example: It is ironic that the lifeguard fears water.
The writer supports each claim with facts.
Choose only relevant evidence.
The essay answers a counterargument.

words.definition: to reach a conclusion from evidence
words.example: We can infer the character feels nervous.
Use the graph to justify your answer.

themes.title: Forces of Change
themes.description: Words for causes, effects, and turning points

words.example: Some seasonal change is inevitable.
The resilient community rebuilt.
One event can trigger a larger movement.

words.definition: important or large enough to notice
words.example: The law created significant change.
Every choice has a consequence.

themes.title: Research & Credibility
themes.description: Words for evaluating sources and supporting conclusions

words.example: A second record can corroborate the account.

words.definition: a preference that can distort judgment
words.example: Check the article for possible bias.

words.definition: a reference to the source of information
words.example: Add a citation for the statistic.
A reliable source explains its evidence.

words.definition: to restate an idea in your own words
words.example: Paraphrase the finding without changing its meaning.

themes.title: Character & Theme
themes.description: Words for tracing motivation and meaning in literature

words.example: Fear is the character's main motivation.

words.definition: made of connected parts and not simple
words.example: The novel presents a complex conflict.

words.definition: to hint at what will happen later
words.example: The broken clock may foreshadow trouble.
The hero faces a moral dilemma.

words.definition: a central message explored by a text
words.example: Courage is one theme of the story.

themes.title: Rhetoric & Voice
themes.description: Words for analyzing how language persuades

words.example: The speech uses powerful rhetoric.

words.definition: a feeling or idea suggested by a word
words.example: The word home has a warm connotation.
Her tone adds nuance to the statement.
The article cites credible sources.
Repetition can emphasize a key idea.

themes.title: Complex Systems
themes.description: Words for patterns, structures, and analysis

words.example: The parts of an ecosystem are interdependent.
Temperature was the tested variable.

words.definition: to combine ideas into a new whole
words.example: Synthesize both sources in your response.
Time was the main design constraint.
Analyze the pattern before deciding.

themes.title: Academic Argument
themes.description: Precise language for reasoning and persuasion

words.example: The author asserts that the policy will help.

words.definition: to limit a statement by adding conditions
words.example: The writer must qualify the broad claim.

words.definition: to prove a claim false with evidence
words.example: Use the data to refute the misconception.
The essay presents a coherent argument.

words.definition: a possible result or suggested meaning
words.example: Consider the long-term implication of the choice.

themes.title: Innovation & Ethics
themes.description: Words for discussing technology, choices, and responsibility

words.definition: consistent with principles of right conduct
words.example: The class debated the ethical use of data.
Good systems include public accountability.
Researchers must obtain informed consent.

words.definition: fair while accounting for different needs
words.example: The team searched for an equitable solution.
The design had an unintended consequence.

references.note: CC BY curriculum sequence and lesson contexts
